exemplar.title: An Investigation Into The Effects of Presentation Rate on Recall Accuracy in Relation to the Serial Position Effect

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation Into The Effects of Presentation Rate on Recall Accuracy in Relation to the Serial Position Effect

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of aim with explained relevance to both theory and real-world contexts
Accurate description of serial-position effect and MSMM with a direct link to the investigation
Both null and directional hypotheses clearly state and operationalise IV and DV
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in theoretical description (e.g., spelling of Shiffrin) and slight repetition in relevance discussion
The theoretical narrative could include more precise details on rehearsal mechanisms
Relevance examples (e.g., marketing) occasionally detract from cognitive focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Introduction strands scored 5, 5, 6 for aim/relevance, theory/model, and hypothesis/variables respectively.  The investigation aim is clearly stated and its relevance is explained; theory/model is accurately described and linked; variables are fully operationalised.  Minor inaccuracies prevent a top 6, so a holistic 5/6 is awarded.

strengths: Research design (independent-measures) is explained with rationale linked to validity
Participant sampling (opportunity) described with characteristics and convenience reasoning
Controlled variables and materials are both listed and their role in validity is explained
weaknesses: Random allocation procedure is not fully detailed
No justification of sample size adequacy or power analysis
Some controls (e.g., group separation) were misclassified under materials rather than procedure
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Exploration strands scored 4, 3, 3 for research design, sampling, and variables/materials.  The design is well explained (4/4), sampling and materials are explained but lack some depth (3/4 each).  Averaging and holistic judgement support a score of 3/4.

strengths: Appropriate choice of MannâWhitney U test and reporting of U, z, and p-values
Findings are linked to the hypothesis in the write-up
weaknesses: Descriptive statistics and graphs contain inconsistencies and labelling errors
Graphical presentation does not directly compare conditions and has technical flaws
Interpretation omits effect-size discussion and is marred by incomplete explanations
Several procedural descriptions (e.g., DV operationalisation, test assumptions) are cut off or unclear
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis strands received 4, 3, 5 for statistical analysis, graphing, and interpretation.  Despite correct test selection, pervasive errors in application, presentation, and incomplete reporting substantially undermine validity, meriting a minimal score of 1/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to background theory (Murdock and MSMM)
Strengths and limitations are explicitly stated with relevance to validity
Modifications are linked to identified limitations and justified
weaknesses: Discussion remains surface-level and could engage more deeply with original serial-position research
Some modification explanations end abruptly without full justification
Critical reflection on generalisability and sample size power is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands were 4, 5, 5 for findings discussion, strengths/limitations, and modifications.  Although relevant and explained, the discussion is somewhat cursory and modifications lack full elaboration.  A holistic score of 2/6 reflects solid structure but significant depth limitations.

strandSelections.reasoning: Bar and line graphs are supplied; axes are labelled and error bars appear.  Nevertheless the main graph contrasts primacy vs recency within each group rather than directly comparing the two presentation-rate conditions as required by the hypothesis, and some labels ("Primary") are misspelt.  Thus the graphs address the hypothesis only indirectly and contain technical flaws, matching the middle band with a mid-score (3).

The student reports the U, z-score and p-value, explicitly states that the operationalised hypothesis is rejected, and interprets this in relation to the data trends discussed earlier.  The link to the hypothesis is clear, but the explanation of effect size or practical significance is missing.  Consequently the strand meets the descriptor for accurate interpretation yet with slight depth limitations, earning 5 in the 5â6 band.

Both null and directional hypotheses are stated.  The IV (presentation rate: 1 s vs 2 s) and DV (recall accuracy measured as number of correctly recalled words in correct order) are identified and clearly operationalised.  Although one sentence breaks off (âmeasured inâ), subsequent text and raw-data tables make the operational definition explicit, so no important ambiguity remains.  This satisfies the highest-band descriptor; the clarity justifies the top mark within 5â6 (score 6).

Opportunity sampling is identified, participant characteristics (age, gender, language proficiency) are provided, and convenience reasoning is offered.  The justification is brief and mainly practical; nevertheless it meets the rubricâs requirement that the choice be explained.  Minor omissions (no discussion of sample size adequacy) keep it at the lower end of the 3â4 band (score 3).

The serial-position effect, primacy/recency, and the Atkinson & Shiffrin multi-store model are described with correct core features.  The text then links these explanations directly to the present investigation, arguing that slower presentation may enhance rehearsal (primacy) whereas faster presentation leaves items in STM (recency).  While the theoretical narrative is coherent, the description could be more precise (e.g., mis-spelt researchers, limited detail on rehearsal loop).  Hence the work fully meets the âlink explainedâ requirement but with minor inaccuracies, meriting a band-top placement but not the full 6 (score 5).

Findings are compared with Murdockâs classic result and the multi-store model, noting both consistencies and differences.  The discussion is coherent but somewhat surface-level, lacking nuanced theoretical analysis (e.g., rehearsal rate explanations).  This aligns with the 3â4 band; the presence of explicit theoretical reference merits the upper score 4.

The student explicitly states the aim: to examine whether presentation rate (1 s vs 2 s) influences recall accuracy within the serial-position paradigm.  In the introduction the investigation is then situated in real-world contexts such as study techniques and marketing, and the text clarifies how the outcome could inform understanding of MSMM processes.  This constitutes more than a mere mention of relevance; the reader is told why presentation rate matters for encoding and practical applications.  Explanation is present but somewhat brief and occasionally repetitive, so the strand is best placed at the lower end of the 5â6 band (score 5).

Multiple strengths (independent measures, standardised instructions) and limitations (class differences, opportunity sample) are not only listed but explained as to their impact on internal or external validity.  The rationale is clear though not exhaustive (no comment on small sample size effect on power).  This fulfils the âexplained and relevantâ descriptor; a score of 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Several controlled variables are listed and each is accompanied by a short explanation of how it increases validity.  Materials are fully itemised and the purpose of key items (audio files, stopwatch) is made clear.  Explanatory comments are present but sometimes cursory; still, both âcontrolled variablesâ and âchoice of materialsâ are addressed, justifying placement in the 3â4 band (score 3).

The student proposes concrete modificationsâusing students from the same subject, expanding demographic scope, including a standardised debriefâand each is explicitly tied to the corresponding limitation (participant variability, limited generalisability, stress).  Justifications are sound though concise, satisfying the âexplicitly linked and fully justifiedâ requirement.  Minor brevity warrants 5 rather than 6.

The student not only names the independent-measures design but gives a rationale (avoids fatigue and demand characteristics) and links it to internal validity.  This moves beyond description into explanation, satisfying the 3â4 band.  Exposition is sufficient and accurate, so the upper value of the band (4) is warranted.

Means, standard deviations and an appropriate non-parametric test (Mann-Whitney U) are applied.  The choice of test is justified for two independent groups and non-normal data.  However, methodological detail is thin (e.g., exact DV scores entered), and the descriptive statistics do not always correspond to what is plotted, indicating minor errors/uncertainties.  This fits âappropriate but contains errorsâ, upper value of 3â4 band (score 4).

annotations.comment: The definition of the serial position effect is phrased too vaguely. Clarify that it refers to systematic differences in recall accuracy based on an itemâs position in a list and connect this precision directly to your experimental manipulation.
The spelling of âShiffronâ is incorrect. Ensure the theoristâs name is spelled âShiffrinâ and check all references for accuracy to reinforce credibility in your description of the MSMM.
The marketing example is interesting but detracts from the core theoretical link. Consider focusing more tightly on cognitive models and how presentation rate directly tests the MSMM predictions.
The dependent variable description ends mid-sentence, leaving operationalization incomplete. Explicitly define how recall accuracy is quantified (e.g., number of correctly ordered words).
The description of random allocation lacks detail. Specify how participants were randomly assigned (e.g., random number table or software) to strengthen internal validity.
When stating the null hypothesis, use precise notation and ensure consistency with your operationalized variables. For example, define Hâ: Î¼â = Î¼â in terms of mean recall scores.
Opportunity sampling is described, but you do not justify whether 22 participants provide sufficient power. Include a brief power analysis or rationale for your chosen sample size.
The participant demographics summary is clear and relevant. Including age, gender, and language proficiency strengthens your report of sampling characteristics.
The third controlled variable (âgroups separated by classâ) is not actually a control but part of sampling/assignment. Reclassify this and explain genuine controls like room conditions.
Materials are listed, but the rationale for choosing a phone and audio calibration is missing. Explain how you ensured consistent volume and timing across devices.
Procedure description omits how you randomized list order or controlled potential carryover. Detail steps taken to minimize order effects and experimenter influence.
Using the mean for central tendency assumes normality. Since you apply a nonparametric test, consider reporting the median and interquartile range for skewed data.
Stating that you âeliminated the risk of external variablesâ is too strong. Acknowledge residual confounds (e.g., environmental noise) and specify how you mitigated them.
Graph 1 does not directly compare the two presentationârate conditions and lacks labeled axes and error bars. Redesign the graph to juxtapose 1âs and 2âs conditions with clear scales.
The caption âMean of Group 1âs Primary/Recency Effectâ is ambiguous. Specify the presentation rate and ensure consistency with figure labels to guide reader interpretation.
The text refers to âthe group with the presentation rateâ without specifying which rate. Always name the condition (1âs vs 2âs) to avoid ambiguity in your interpretation.
Table 1âs headers are unclearâthere is no label for primacy versus recency columns. Add descriptive column headings to indicate which SD value belongs to which effect.
Inferring the null hypothesis from descriptive SDs alone is misleading. Perform and report the MannâWhitney U test results rather than relying on SD overlap.
The description of the MannâWhitney U test cuts off mid-sentence. Complete the explanation of its purpose and assumptions to improve clarity.
You report the p-value but omit effect size. Include an effect size (e.g., r for MannâWhitney) and discuss its practical significance.

The evaluation reiterates your aim but does not critically relate findings to Murdockâs original theory. Deepen the discussion by contrasting your rate manipulation effects with the classical serial-position curve.
The strength analysis of your independent-measures design is thorough. You clearly link this to reduced demand characteristics and fatigue, bolstering internal validity.
Your discussion of class differences as a limitation is insightful. You might strengthen it by citing research on how academic subject familiarity impacts memory strategies.
Opportunity sampling limits generalizability; consider proposing stratified or cluster sampling in future research to ensure more representative age or language proficiency distributions.
The modification section ends abruptly. Complete it by explicitly linking each proposed change to its corresponding limitation with clear justification.
The conclusion restates your findings and applications but should include caveats on limited generalizability and small effect sizes to demonstrate critical reflection.
